news_summary: Today's environmental news highlights significant progress in urban greening, coral reef resilience, the rapid expansion of renewable energy surpassing electricity demand growth, and pioneering policies banning fossil fuel advertising.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées significatives en matière de verdissement urbain, de résilience des récifs coralliens, de l'expansion rapide des énergies renouvelables qui dépasse la croissance de la demande en électricité, ainsi que des mesures pionnières visant à interdire la publicité pour les combustibles fossiles.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan avances significativos en la ecologización urbana, la resiliencia de los arrecifes de coral, la rápida expansión de las energías renovables, que supera el crecimiento de la demanda eléctrica, y las políticas pioneras que prohíben la publicidad de los combustibles fósiles.

articles.title: The huge, untapped potential of planting rooftop gardens in cities
articles.content:
The city has long been a beacon of opportunity, where folks flock to make it big. But metropolises the world over are wasting a major opportunity — many, many square feet of it: Flat rooftops are painted white, when really they should be green.

Not, mind you, shades of mint green, forest green, or lime green, but with the lushness of actual plants. Adding vegetation to roofs — even if it’s just a coating of grass, moss, and succulents — bestows many overlapping, reinforcing benefits not only on a building’s occupants and owner, but on the surrounding community. Like parks on the ground, gardens in the sky reduce local temperatures and help prevent flooding, all while improving urban biodiversity and feeding pollinators like bees.

According to a recent report prepared for the European Commission, the executive body of the European Union, if cities accelerated the transformation of these unused spaces into oases — and converted empty walls into vertical yet verdant surfaces — they’d make themselves more comfortable for urbanites as temperatures climb. Burgs might even start growing crops under solar panels, a burgeoning field known as rooftop agrivoltaics, simultaneously generating food and electricity. The technique could be especially powerful as urban populations continue to balloon: According to the United Nations, another 2 billion people could be living in cities by 2050. At the same time, a phenomenon called the urban heat island effect, in which the built environment warms much more than surrounding rural areas, is driving temperatures to increasingly dangerous levels.

“Our goal is to get our cities more dense, but keep them livable and climate-safe,” said Vera Enzi-Zechner, co-lead author of the report and a vice president at the European Federation of Green Roof and Living Wall Associations, based in Vienna, Austria. “Of course, also water comes in, energy comes in, multi-functionality, social cohesion, engagement, and biodiversity.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Even simple vegetation, like grasses and mosses, turns a roof into a thriving ecosystem. Courtesy Over Easy Solar

The green roof is a surprisingly old technology. Consider the Moos Water Filtration Plant, near Zurich, whose rooftops have hosted nine acres of meadows for over a century. Less of a planned amenity and more of a serendipitous colonization of plant life, the rooftops have nevertheless transformed into refuges for native species.

These days, architects would rather not have their buildings bloom by accident. Instead, they’re incorporating the requisite infrastructure into the design. To keep the garden from leaking into the top floor, for instance, they add waterproofing and barriers to contain roots. Designers also must consider the additional weight of the plants and water that soaks into the substrate. How much weight, exactly, will depend on what they want to grow: It might be simple grasses and mosses, like at the Moos Water Filtration Plant, or shrubs, or even trees, whose roots require a thicker layer. And it’s never too late to do this. An owner can add a green roof after construction is finished, though it might require reinforcement.

The investment, though, could pay serious dividends over time. Whereas a traditional roof absorbs the sun’s energy, a green one sweats like a human, as its plants release water vapor during photosynthesis and shade the surface. This cools the air, and the substrate and waterproofing insulate the top floor. Beyond reducing cooling and heating costs, the greenery can extend the life of a roof, the report notes, because relentless sunlight, extreme heat waves, and hail aren’t aging shingles and paint and such. (Traditional roofs are constantly stressed by expanding and shrinking, as the sun heats them during the day and the night cools them off.) Down at street level, building owners can cover walls with vegetation, protecting them from the elements and providing habitat for birds and bats.

Cities, too, could save money by embracing gardening in the sky. As the planet heats up and rainfall gets more intense, gutters and sewers — designed for the climate of yesteryear — are struggling to keep up. Green roofs soak up some of that deluge and slow the flow of water into these systems, preventing costly flooding. Some designers are even going a step further with blue-green roofs, which incorporate systems that store rainwater to flush the toilets inside.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Green roofs, like here in Hamburg, Germany, provide stepping stones for flying animals to move across the urban landscape.

Markus Scholz / Picture Alliance via Getty Images

So instead of all that precipitation flowing into sewers and out to sea, some of it goes instead to plants and people. “That is the basic principle for improved resiliency for many cities around the world,” said Steven Peck, founder and president of the nonprofit industry association Green Roofs for Healthy Cities. “We’ve got to capture that water and use it to support plants, because plants will do good things for us, like clean the air. They’re good for our mental health. They’ll cool the city.”

Beyond reducing temperatures and flooding, all this additional greenery creates much-needed habitat for local plants and animals. It’s all well and good to have parks and gardens dotting a city, but this creates better pathways for species. Ideally, green roofs and walls would form a citywide network of stepping stones for flying creatures to move around safely, settle down in shelter, and tap reliable supplies of food. Indeed, the report notes that study after study has shown that this not only boosts biodiversity, it provides havens for endangered and rare species of flora and fauna. “We cannot just integrate green roofs or green walls into the urban environment,” said Maria Manso, co-lead author of the report and an assistant professor of engineering at Lusófona University in Lisbon, Portugal. “They have to be connected with other nature-based solutions in order to have this urban connectivity.”

Free-roaming insects and birds will support the next frontier of green roofs: Scientists are now trying to figure out what crops might grow well up there, under the shade of solar panels. These rooftop agrivoltaic systems shelter the plants from fierce winds and excessive sunlight, and in turn, the vegetation releases water vapor, cooling the panels above and increasing their efficiency, meaning they can generate more electricity. Early research is finding that warm-season crops in particular, like watermelon, do astonishingly well high in the sky, with cucumbers growing as big as baseball bats. The fliers visiting these plants may well travel to nearby urban gardens on the ground and pollinate crops there, or even head to traditional agricultural fields abutting the city.

Corn grows on the roof of Colorado State University, where scientists are researching which crops might thrive high off the ground. Courtesy Kevin Samuelson, CSU Spur

The early results with rooftop agrivoltaics are so promising, in fact, that projects are popping up around the world. In Italy, for instance, the Florence airport’s roof will soon host solar panels and a vineyard. “They couldn’t decide between renewable energy generation or their cultural heritage of growing grapes,” said horticulturist Jennifer Bousselot, who studies rooftop agrivoltaics at Colorado State University but wasn’t involved in the report. “So they decided to actually combine the two.”

While photovoltaic panels increase the upfront cost, they too can pay dividends, and not just with the energy they provide. Because their shade reduces evaporation, rooftop gardeners need to apply less water. That could also mean that a designer might not need as thick a layer of soil to retain water, if less of it is evaporating away. “So in theory, you can actually have a shallower green roof if you’ve got shade integrated, and still have high diversity and high plant success,” Bousselot said.

Given all these benefits, how can cities encourage the proliferation of verdant roofs and walls? The report points out that starting decades ago, Basel, Switzerland, provided subsidies for greening the city’s roofs, then changed its building codes to mandate that all new and retrofitted flat roofs get the treatment. By 2010, the city had increased its green roof surface area from 10 to 100 hectares (247 acres). “This tenfold increase,” the report notes, “positioned Basel as a leading example of how coordinated policy, financial support, and ecological standards can effectively accelerate the adoption of biodiversity-friendly green roofs in urban environments.”

In the United States, cities set their own building codes, so they could do much the same. A growing number of municipalities in the U.S. are also starting to charge property owners for the rainfall runoff they produce: The more impervious surfaces you have, the more you pay. Rip out more concrete, though, and install gardens high and low, and you can reduce those fees.

This being American real estate, developers will want to know that even in the absence of subsidies, the things can make money over time, whereas traditional roofs lose it because they have to be replaced within decades. Buildings can even rent out their verdant rooftops for private events.

Cities the world over stand at an inflection point: Invest now in as much green space wherever they can get it, or risk withering as the world warms. “They’re going to be healthier places to live in the face of ongoing climate change impacts,” Peck said. “And that’s where the money is going to be. That’s where the creativity is going to be.”
articles.reasoning: The article details the significant environmental and societal benefits of transforming urban rooftops into green spaces, highlighting measurable impacts such as temperature reduction, flood prevention, biodiversity enhancement, and urban sustainability. It discusses scalable solutions with broad relevance to global urban populations, supported by concrete examples and policy successes, making it a substantive and inspiring story about climate action and urban resilience.
articles.personality_title: Cities find new green potential by turning rooftops into gardens
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cities around the world have many flat rooftops that are mostly bare or painted white. These spaces are often unused, missing a chance to help with problems like heat, flooding, and lack of nature in busy urban areas.

**What happened** – A recent report by the European Commission shows that turning rooftops and building walls into green spaces with plants, grass, and gardens can bring many benefits. These green roofs cool buildings, reduce flooding by soaking up rainwater, and create homes for insects and birds. Some places are even growing crops on rooftops under solar panels to produce food and clean energy at the same time.

**Impact** – Green roofs help cities handle challenges like rising temperatures and heavy rain caused by climate change. They make buildings last longer by protecting them from sun damage and reduce energy costs by keeping buildings cooler. They also connect small natural areas in cities, helping plants and animals survive. Cities like Basel in Switzerland have shown that with support and rules, green roofs can grow quickly and improve urban life.

**What's next step** – More cities might follow Basel’s example by offering money or making laws to encourage green roofs and walls. Researchers are exploring which crops grow best on rooftops, especially under solar panels, to make urban farming more common. This could lead to healthier cities that use space wisely and support nature.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Turning city rooftops into green gardens cools buildings, prevents floods, supports wildlife, and could help feed growing urban populations sustainably.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les villes exploitent le potentiel des jardins sur les toits
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les villes du monde entier possèdent de nombreux toits plats souvent inutilisés ou simplement peints en blanc. Ces espaces représentent une occasion manquée d’aider à résoudre des problèmes comme la chaleur, les inondations et le manque de nature en milieu urbain.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un rapport récent de la Commission européenne montre que transformer les toits et les murs des bâtiments en espaces verts avec des plantes, de l’herbe et des jardins apporte de nombreux avantages. Ces toits végétalisés rafraîchissent les bâtiments, réduisent les inondations en absorbant l’eau de pluie et créent des habitats pour les insectes et les oiseaux. Certains endroits cultivent même des légumes sur les toits sous des panneaux solaires pour produire de la nourriture et de l’énergie propre.

**Impact** – Les toits verts aident les villes à faire face aux défis du changement climatique, comme la hausse des températures et les fortes pluies. Ils protègent les bâtiments du soleil, prolongent leur durée de vie et réduisent les coûts d’énergie en les gardant plus frais. Ils relient aussi des espaces naturels en ville, aidant la faune et la flore à survivre. Des villes comme Bâle en Suisse montrent qu’avec des aides et des règles, les toits verts peuvent se développer rapidement.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres villes pourraient suivre l’exemple de Bâle en offrant des subventions ou en créant des lois pour encourager les toits et murs végétalisés. Les chercheurs étudient quels légumes poussent le mieux sur les toits, notamment sous les panneaux solaires. Cela pourrait rendre l’agriculture urbaine plus courante et créer des villes plus saines qui utilisent l’espace intelligemment.

**En une phrase** – Transformer les toits des villes en jardins verts rafraîchit les bâtiments, prévient les inondations, soutient la biodiversité et pourrait aider à nourrir durablement les populations urbaines croissantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Las ciudades descubren el potencial de los jardines en las azoteas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las ciudades de todo el mundo tienen muchas azoteas planas que suelen estar vacías o pintadas de blanco. Estos espacios a menudo no se usan y pierden la oportunidad de ayudar con problemas como el calor, las inundaciones y la falta de naturaleza en áreas urbanas.

**Qué pasó** – Un informe reciente de la Comisión Europea muestra que convertir azoteas y paredes de edificios en espacios verdes con plantas, pasto y jardines trae muchos beneficios. Estos techos verdes enfrían los edificios, reducen inundaciones al absorber el agua de lluvia y crean hogares para insectos y aves. Algunos lugares incluso cultivan alimentos en azoteas bajo paneles solares para producir comida y energía limpia al mismo tiempo.

**Impacto** – Los techos verdes ayudan a las ciudades a enfrentar desafíos como el aumento de temperaturas y lluvias intensas causadas por el cambio climático. Protegen los edificios del sol, prolongan su vida útil y reducen los costos de energía al mantenerlos frescos. También conectan pequeños espacios naturales en la ciudad, ayudando a plantas y animales a sobrevivir. Ciudades como Basilea en Suiza han demostrado que con apoyo y reglas, los techos verdes pueden crecer rápido y mejorar la vida urbana.

**Próximo paso** – Más ciudades podrían seguir el ejemplo de Basilea ofreciendo dinero o creando leyes para fomentar techos y paredes verdes. Los investigadores exploran qué cultivos crecen mejor en azoteas, especialmente bajo paneles solares, para hacer la agricultura urbana más común. Esto podría llevar a ciudades más saludables que usen el espacio de manera inteligente y apoyen la naturaleza.

**Resumen en una frase** – Convertir las azoteas de la ciudad en jardines verdes enfría edificios, previene inundaciones, apoya la biodiversidad y podría ayudar a alimentar a poblaciones urbanas crecientes de forma sostenible.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant cityscape viewed from above, where flat rooftops transform into lush gardens with thriving patches of grass, moss, succulents, and small crops growing beneath solar panels, while flying bees and birds gently move between green walls and rooftop meadows, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a detailed, painterly style.

articles.title: Coral reefs on a remote archipelago shrugged off a massive heatwave
articles.summary: Scientists were shocked to find that the Houtman Abrolhos Islands’ coral reefs survived a prolonged extreme heatwave in 2025 virtually unharmed, which may reveal how to protect corals elsewhere
articles.content:
Scientists were shocked to find that the Houtman Abrolhos Islands’ coral reefs survived a prolonged extreme heatwave in 2025 virtually unharmed, which may reveal how to protect corals elsewhere

The Houtman Abrolhos Islands, off Western Australia, where corals appear to be exceptionally heat-tolerant Bill Bachman/Alamy

Coral reefs on a chain of islands off Western Australia were almost untouched by a prolonged heatwave that devastated corals in other regions in early 2025. Researchers hope that learning the secret of extreme heat tolerance in these corals will help to protect reefs across the globe, which are in danger of being wiped out by global warming.

Kate Quigley at the University of Western Australia in Perth and her colleagues dived at 11 sites across the Houtman Abrolhos archipelago in July 2025.

Advertisement

Further north at the Ningaloo Reef, up to 60 per cent of corals died during the same heatwave. This was a story repeated at reefs around the world, with marine heatwaves in 2025 killing vast swathes of coral globally.

But at Houtman Abrolhos, apart from a few tiny patches, there weren’t even any signs of stress, such as fluorescing coral. “We expected to see mass bleaching with lots of white colonies, and likely mortality of reefs, given we did surveys after many months of marine heatwave. We did not see this,” says Quigley.

Prolonged heat stress generally leads to coral bleaching, when corals expel the symbiotic algae that live in their tissues, which provide most of their food.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

Researchers measure the heat stress faced by corals in degree heating weeks (DHW), which accounts for how long a heatwave endures and how high temperatures reach.

Over 4 °C-weeks, scientists expect to see significant bleaching and above 8 °C-weeks, the situation becomes dire. “Values of around 8 °C-weeks are generally considered catastrophic and are often associated with widespread bleaching and mortality,” says Quigley.

The waters around the Houtman Abrolhos Islands hit 4 °C-weeks in early February 2025 and 8 °C-weeks by early March, but the temperatures kept rising and by mid-April the corals had experienced 22 °C-weeks of heat stress.

Quigley and her colleagues were most surprised to find that the full array of coral species at the reef all seemed immune to what had proved disastrous elsewhere.

To try to determine just how heat-tolerant the coral at the Houtman Abrolhos Islands actually are, the scientists brought colonies from several species back to the lab and subjected them to prolonged high temperatures.

At 8 °C-weeks, compared with currently accepted thresholds, survival rates at the Houtman Abrolhos islands were twice as high and bleaching resistance was nearly four times higher. There was still nearly 100 per cent survival at around 16 °C-weeks.

While the upper limit of the tolerance of corals there is still unclear, it is “clearly substantial and higher than what has been documented at other reef locations studied so far around the world”, says Quigley.

The next step for the researchers is to work out exactly how the corals are achieving this survival feat.

Because resistance was across many species, Quigley says it is possibly the algal symbionts that are giving the Houtman Abrolhos island corals their superpower.

“I think this location has a particular set of environmental factors that has driven the evolution of heat tolerance generally for the species that live there,” she says. Because of this, such reefs should be given the highest level of protection, and other similar high-tolerance sites should also be identified, she says.

Petra Lundgren at the Great Barrier Reef Foundation says such reefs serve as “natural laboratories for understanding heat tolerance”.

“They may also hold the key to advancing selective breeding and other interventions aimed at enhancing thermal resilience in conservation aquaculture and coral restoration,” says Lundgren.

While focusing on curbing global carbon emissions remains the most critical action to save these precious ecosystems, “providing adaptive assistance by, for example, seeding reefs with more heat-tolerant corals will give coral reefs their best chance at adapting to future heat stress events,” she says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that coral reefs at the Houtman Abrolhos Islands survived an extreme heatwave that devastated other reefs globally. This finding has broad implications for coral conservation and climate change adaptation, offering hope for protecting vulnerable ecosystems worldwide. The article provides detailed context on the event, scientific measurements, and potential future applications, meeting criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: Coral reefs off Western Australia survive extreme heatwave, offering clues to protect others
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In early 2025, many coral reefs around the world suffered severe damage due to a long and intense heatwave. Coral bleaching, caused by heat stress, kills corals by making them lose the tiny algae that provide them food.

**What happened** – Scientists studied coral reefs at the Houtman Abrolhos Islands, a remote group of islands off Western Australia, in July 2025. Despite experiencing very high heat stress for months, these corals showed almost no damage, unlike other reefs nearby and around the world that were badly harmed.

**Impact** – The corals at Houtman Abrolhos survived heat levels much higher than what usually causes death or bleaching. This is unusual because the heatwave was strong enough to kill up to 60% of corals at other places like Ningaloo Reef. Understanding how these corals survive could help researchers find ways to protect other reefs from climate change.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to study why these corals tolerate heat so well. They think it could be due to special algae living inside the corals or unique local conditions. This knowledge might lead to new methods like breeding heat-resistant corals or helping reefs adapt to future heatwaves.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Coral reefs at the Houtman Abrolhos Islands survived a severe 2025 heatwave, revealing new possibilities for protecting vulnerable reefs worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les récifs coralliens au large de l’Australie-Occidentale résistent à une vague de chaleur extrême, offrant des pistes pour protéger d’autres récifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au début de 2025, de nombreux récifs coralliens dans le monde ont été gravement endommagés par une longue et intense vague de chaleur. Le blanchissement corallien, causé par le stress thermique, tue les coraux en les privant des petites algues qui leur fournissent de la nourriture.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juillet 2025, des scientifiques ont étudié les récifs coralliens des îles Houtman Abrolhos, un groupe d’îles isolées au large de l’Australie-Occidentale. Malgré un stress thermique très élevé pendant plusieurs mois, ces coraux n’ont presque pas été endommagés, contrairement à d’autres récifs proches et dans le monde entier.

**Impact** – Les coraux des Houtman Abrolhos ont survécu à des températures bien supérieures à celles qui causent normalement la mort ou le blanchissement. C’est inhabituel car la vague de chaleur a tué jusqu’à 60 % des coraux dans des endroits comme le récif Ningaloo. Comprendre comment ces coraux survivent pourrait aider à protéger d’autres récifs face au changement climatique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre pourquoi ces coraux tolèrent si bien la chaleur. Ils pensent que cela vient peut-être d’algues spéciales vivant dans les coraux ou des conditions locales uniques. Ces connaissances pourraient permettre de nouvelles méthodes, comme élever des coraux résistants à la chaleur ou aider les récifs à s’adapter aux futures vagues de chaleur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les récifs coralliens des îles Houtman Abrolhos ont survécu à une vague de chaleur sévère en 2025, ouvrant des possibilités pour protéger les récifs vulnérables dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Los arrecifes de coral frente a Australia Occidental resisten ola de calor extrema, ofreciendo pistas para proteger otros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A principios de 2025, muchos arrecifes de coral en el mundo sufrieron daños graves por una ola de calor larga e intensa. El blanqueamiento coralino, causado por el estrés térmico, mata a los corales al hacer que pierdan las pequeñas algas que les proporcionan alimento.

**Qué pasó** – En julio de 2025, científicos estudiaron los arrecifes de coral en las islas Houtman Abrolhos, un grupo remoto frente a Australia Occidental. A pesar de un estrés térmico muy alto durante meses, estos corales casi no mostraron daños, a diferencia de otros arrecifes cercanos y en el mundo.

**Impacto** – Los corales en Houtman Abrolhos sobrevivieron a niveles de calor mucho mayores de los que normalmente causan muerte o blanqueamiento. Esto es raro porque la ola de calor mató hasta el 60% de los corales en lugares como el arrecife Ningaloo. Entender cómo sobreviven estos corales podría ayudar a proteger otros arrecifes del cambio climático.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren descubrir por qué estos corales toleran tan bien el calor. Piensan que puede ser por algas especiales dentro de los corales o por condiciones locales únicas. Este conocimiento podría llevar a métodos nuevos, como criar corales resistentes al calor o ayudar a los arrecifes a adaptarse a futuras olas de calor.

**Frase clave** – Los arrecifes de coral en las islas Houtman Abrolhos sobrevivieron a una fuerte ola de calor en 2025, mostrando nuevas posibilidades para proteger arrecifes vulnerables en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant underwater scene of a flourishing coral reef at the Houtman Abrolhos Islands, with diverse coral species glowing softly in warm, natural hues amid clear, calm waters, symbolizing resilience and strength against intense heat stress.

articles.title: 2025: The year renewables finally outpaced global electricity demand growth
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Something shifted in the world’s energy system in 2025, and the numbers are hard to argue with. For the first time in modern history, clean energy generation grew faster than global electricity demand, meaning every new watt of power the world needed last year came from renewables, not coal, […]
The post 2025: The year renewables finally outpaced global electricity demand growth first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Something shifted in the world’s energy system in 2025, and the numbers are hard to argue with.

For the first time in modern history, clean energy generation grew faster than global electricity demand, meaning every new watt of power the world needed last year came from renewables, not coal, oil, or gas. Fossil fuel generation, which had risen in nearly every year of this century, fell by 0.2 percent.

The data comes from Ember, an energy think tank that tracks electricity output from 215 countries. Clean power generation grew 887 terawatt-hours in 2025, exceeding global demand growth of 849 terawatt-hours. The share of renewables in the global electricity mix crossed one-third for the first time, reaching 33.8 percent.

Solar did most of the work, growing 30 percent and meeting three-quarters of last year’s net rise in demand on its own. Combined with wind, the two sources covered 99 percent of new demand growth.

China and India changed the math

For the first time this century, both China and India saw declines in fossil fuel generation. China fell 0.9 percent. India fell 3.3 percent. Both countries, long among the world’s largest fossil fuel consumers, are now, in the words of Ember’s lead analyst Nicolas Fulghum, “aggressively pursuing a strategy of diversification through bringing renewables into the mix.”

China alone accounted for more than half of global solar capacity and generation growth in 2025, and for most of the world’s new wind output. India set records in both solar and wind generation.

“We’re coming from a period over the last few decades where new electricity demand growth meant growth in fossil generation,” said Fulghum. “We’re now moving into a world where that’s no longer the case.”

Storage is making it stick

Battery costs fell 45 percent last year while storage capacity grew 46 percent, allowing solar power to run beyond peak daylight hours. Ember estimates that the battery capacity added in 2025 can shift 14 percent of solar generation from midday to other parts of the day.

The US and Europe each added substantial new solar capacity, 85 terawatt-hours and 60 terawatt-hours, respectively, even as fossil fuels saw small increases in both markets. In the US, political pressure to expand coal, oil, and gas production has not reversed the broader trend.

Alexis Abramson, dean of the Columbia University Climate School, said the shift is real. “We’ve really crossed this important threshold that clean energy now can meet rising demand economically and at the same time really help address national security concerns,” she said. “The next challenge is really turning that into a steady decline of fossil fuel use as well.”

Clean energy can now keep pace with demand growth. Replacing the fossil fuel generation that already exists is a different, harder task, one that will take longer and will require this pace of deployment to hold for years. The 2025 numbers are a starting point that the world has not had before.

This solution is highlighted by The World Business Academy, the umbrella organization producing The Optimist Daily. To learn more, please visit our website.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant milestone in global energy transition where renewable energy generation outpaced global electricity demand growth for the first time, indicating a meaningful shift towards cleaner energy sources with broad environmental and societal benefits. It provides detailed data on renewable growth, fossil fuel decline, and regional contributions, highlighting a substantial positive impact on climate action.
articles.personality_title: Renewable energy beats global electricity demand growth for the first time in 2025
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In recent years, the world’s need for electricity has been growing fast. Usually, this extra power came from burning fossil fuels like coal, oil, and gas, which harm the environment. But in 2025, something changed in how we produce electricity.

**What happened** – In 2025, clean energy sources like solar and wind produced more new electricity than the total increase in global demand. This means every extra watt of power needed last year came from renewables, not fossil fuels. According to Ember, a group that studies energy use around the world, renewable energy grew by 887 terawatt-hours, while the global demand only rose by 849 terawatt-hours. Solar energy grew rapidly, covering 75% of this new demand, and together with wind, they met 99% of it. Countries like China and India reduced their fossil fuel use for the first time in decades, with China leading global growth in solar and wind power.

**Impact** – This is the first time in modern history that cleaner energy sources have kept up with or exceeded the world’s growing electricity needs. It shows that renewable energy is becoming a reliable and important part of how the world powers itself. Battery storage technology also improved a lot, helping solar power work even when the sun isn’t shining. This shift means that the world is starting to reduce its dependence on polluting fuels, which could help slow down climate change.

**What’s next step** – While renewables now meet all new electricity demands, replacing the existing fossil fuel plants will take longer. The challenge ahead is to keep increasing clean energy and reduce old fossil fuel use steadily. Governments and companies will likely focus on building more renewable projects and improving storage technology to keep the progress going.

**One-sentence takeaway** – In 2025, renewable energy grew fast enough to supply all new electricity demand worldwide, marking a key step in the shift away from fossil fuels.
articles.personality_title_fr: En 2025, les énergies renouvelables dépassent la croissance mondiale de la demande d’électricité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Ces dernières années, la demande mondiale en électricité a beaucoup augmenté. Habituellement, cette énergie supplémentaire venait de la combustion de combustibles fossiles comme le charbon, le pétrole et le gaz, qui polluent l’environnement. Mais en 2025, la manière dont nous produisons l’électricité a changé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, les énergies propres comme le solaire et l’éolien ont produit plus d’électricité nouvelle que la croissance totale de la demande mondiale. Cela signifie que chaque watt supplémentaire nécessaire l’année dernière venait des renouvelables, pas des combustibles fossiles. Selon Ember, un groupe qui analyse la consommation d’énergie dans le monde, la production d’énergie renouvelable a augmenté de 887 térawattheures, alors que la demande mondiale n’a augmenté que de 849 térawattheures. L’énergie solaire a fortement progressé, couvrant 75 % de cette nouvelle demande, et avec l’éolien, elles ont couvert 99 % de cette croissance. Des pays comme la Chine et l’Inde ont réduit leur utilisation des combustibles fossiles pour la première fois depuis des décennies, la Chine menant la croissance mondiale du solaire et de l’éolien.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois dans l’histoire moderne que les sources d’énergie plus propres suivent ou dépassent la croissance des besoins électriques mondiaux. Cela montre que les énergies renouvelables deviennent une partie fiable et importante de la façon dont le monde s’alimente en électricité. Les progrès dans le stockage par batteries ont aussi beaucoup aidé, permettant au solaire de fonctionner même quand le soleil ne brille pas. Ce changement signifie que le monde commence à réduire sa dépendance aux énergies polluantes, ce qui pourrait aider à ralentir le changement climatique.

**Prochaine étape** – Même si les renouvelables couvrent maintenant toute la nouvelle demande d’électricité, remplacer les centrales à combustibles fossiles existantes prendra plus de temps. Le défi sera de continuer à augmenter la production d’énergie propre et à réduire progressivement l’utilisation des anciennes centrales fossiles. Les gouvernements et entreprises vont probablement se concentrer sur la construction de nouveaux projets renouvelables et l’amélioration des technologies de stockage pour maintenir cette avancée.

**En une phrase** – En 2025, les énergies renouvelables ont crû assez rapidement pour fournir toute la nouvelle demande électrique mondiale, marquant une étape clé dans la transition loin des combustibles fossiles.
articles.personality_title_es: En 2025, las energías renovables superan el crecimiento mundial de la demanda eléctrica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los últimos años, la necesidad mundial de electricidad ha crecido rápidamente. Normalmente, esta energía extra provenía de quemar combustibles fósiles como el carbón, petróleo y gas, que dañan el medio ambiente. Pero en 2025, la forma en que producimos electricidad cambió.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, las energías limpias como la solar y la eólica generaron más electricidad nueva que el aumento total en la demanda mundial. Esto significa que cada vatio extra necesario el año pasado vino de las renovables, no de combustibles fósiles. Según Ember, un grupo que estudia el uso de energía en el mundo, la energía renovable creció 887 teravatios-hora, mientras que la demanda global solo aumentó 849 teravatios-hora. La energía solar creció rápido, cubriendo el 75 % de esta nueva demanda, y junto con la eólica, cubrieron el 99 % de ella. Países como China e India redujeron su uso de combustibles fósiles por primera vez en décadas, con China liderando el crecimiento mundial en solar y eólica.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez en la historia moderna que las fuentes de energía más limpias igualan o superan el crecimiento de la demanda eléctrica mundial. Esto demuestra que las energías renovables se están volviendo una parte confiable e importante de cómo el mundo se abastece de energía. La tecnología de almacenamiento con baterías también mejoró mucho, ayudando a que la energía solar funcione incluso cuando no hay sol. Este cambio significa que el mundo empieza a reducir su dependencia de los combustibles contaminantes, lo que podría ayudar a frenar el cambio climático.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque las renovables ahora cubren toda la nueva demanda eléctrica, reemplazar las plantas existentes de combustibles fósiles tomará más tiempo. El desafío es seguir aumentando la energía limpia y reducir poco a poco el uso de combustibles fósiles viejos. Probablemente, gobiernos y empresas se enfocarán en construir más proyectos renovables y mejorar la tecnología de almacenamiento para continuar con el progreso.

**En una frase** – En 2025, la energía renovable creció lo suficiente para cubrir toda la nueva demanda eléctrica mundial, marcando un paso clave en la transición fuera de los combustibles fósiles.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant landscape at sunrise featuring expansive solar panels and graceful wind turbines stretching across rolling hills, with glowing battery-shaped crystals embedded in the earth symbolizing energy storage, all bathed in warm, natural tones that evoke hope and progress.

articles.title: Amsterdam becomes world's first capital to ban fossil fuel ads
articles.summary: Cities around the globe are clearing their billboards of adverts for flights, cruise ships and petrol cars in a bid to reduce global warming. Amsterdam is the latest city to join the movement, becoming the first capital in the world to approve a legal ban on fossil fuel advertisements. Following the historic win, Dutch activists are now calling for restrictions to be enforced on a national level. FRANCE 24's Valérie Dekimpe and Alexandra Renard report.
articles.content:
Amsterdam becomes world's first capital to ban fossil fuel ads

Europe

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again FOCUS © FRANCE 24 05:37

05:37 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

Cities around the globe are clearing their billboards of adverts for flights, cruise ships and petrol cars in a bid to reduce global warming. Amsterdam is the latest city to join the movement, becoming the first capital in the world to approve a legal ban on fossil fuel advertisements. Following the historic win, Dutch activists are now calling for restrictions to be enforced on a national level. FRANCE 24's Valérie Dekimpe and Alexandra Renard report.
articles.reasoning: Amsterdam's legal ban on fossil fuel advertisements represents a significant and inspiring policy action aimed at combating global warming. As the first capital city to implement such a ban, it sets a meaningful precedent with broad environmental impact and potential to influence other cities and national policies. The article provides clear context about the policy and its implications, highlighting a tangible step toward climate action.
articles.personality_title: Amsterdam becomes first capital to ban fossil fuel ads
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many cities worldwide are removing advertisements for flights, cruise ships, and petrol cars to help fight global warming. This is part of a larger effort to reduce pollution and encourage cleaner energy choices.

**What happened** – Amsterdam has become the first capital city in the world to legally ban advertisements for fossil fuels. This means you will no longer see ads promoting petrol cars or flights on billboards in the city. The decision was approved recently and marks a new step in climate action.

**Impact** – This ban is important because it sets an example for other cities and countries. By stopping fossil fuel ads, Amsterdam is trying to reduce the influence these ads have on people’s choices, encouraging more environmentally friendly habits. It also shows that governments can take real legal steps to fight climate change.

**What's next step** – After this success, activists in the Netherlands want the ban to be applied across the whole country. Other cities might also consider similar bans if they see positive results in Amsterdam.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Amsterdam’s legal ban on fossil fuel advertisements is the first of its kind and could inspire wider actions to protect the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Amsterdam devient la première capitale à interdire les publicités pour les énergies fossiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses villes dans le monde retirent les publicités pour les vols, les croisières et les voitures à essence afin de lutter contre le réchauffement climatique. Cela fait partie d’un effort plus large pour réduire la pollution et encourager les énergies propres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Amsterdam est devenue la première capitale au monde à interdire légalement les publicités pour les énergies fossiles. Cela signifie que les publicités pour les voitures à essence ou les vols ne seront plus visibles sur les panneaux publicitaires de la ville. Cette décision a été récemment approuvée et marque une nouvelle étape dans la lutte contre le changement climatique.

**Impact** – Cette interdiction est importante car elle donne un exemple aux autres villes et pays. En arrêtant les publicités pour les énergies fossiles, Amsterdam cherche à réduire l’influence de ces publicités sur les choix des gens, encourageant des habitudes plus respectueuses de l’environnement. Cela montre aussi que les gouvernements peuvent agir légalement pour combattre le changement climatique.

**Prochaine étape** – Après ce succès, des militants aux Pays-Bas veulent que cette interdiction soit appliquée dans tout le pays. D’autres villes pourraient aussi envisager des interdictions similaires si elles voient des résultats positifs à Amsterdam.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’interdiction légale des publicités pour les énergies fossiles à Amsterdam est une première qui pourrait inspirer des actions plus larges pour protéger l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Ámsterdam se convierte en la primera capital que prohíbe anuncios de combustibles fósiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas ciudades en todo el mundo están retirando anuncios de vuelos, cruceros y coches de gasolina para ayudar a combatir el calentamiento global. Esto forma parte de un esfuerzo mayor para reducir la contaminación y promover energías más limpias.

**Qué pasó** – Ámsterdam se ha convertido en la primera capital del mundo en prohibir legalmente los anuncios de combustibles fósiles. Esto significa que ya no se verán anuncios que promocionen coches de gasolina o vuelos en las vallas publicitarias de la ciudad. La decisión se aprobó recientemente y representa un nuevo paso en la acción climática.

**Impacto** – Esta prohibición es importante porque sirve de ejemplo para otras ciudades y países. Al detener los anuncios de combustibles fósiles, Ámsterdam busca reducir la influencia de estos anuncios en las decisiones de las personas, fomentando hábitos más respetuosos con el medio ambiente. También muestra que los gobiernos pueden tomar medidas legales reales para luchar contra el cambio climático.

**Próximo paso** – Tras este éxito, los activistas en los Países Bajos quieren que la prohibición se aplique en todo el país. Otras ciudades también podrían considerar prohibiciones similares si ven resultados positivos en Ámsterdam.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La prohibición legal de anuncios de combustibles fósiles en Ámsterdam es la primera de su tipo y podría inspirar acciones más amplias para proteger el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Amsterdam cityscape at sunset, where traditional billboards are gently transforming into blooming green plants and clean energy symbols, with silhouettes of wind turbines and bicycles subtly integrated into the background, symbolizing the city’s embrace of fossil fuel ad bans and sustainable living.
